rxn_class_summary: Radical cation Diels–Alder reaction of arylidene cycloalkanes with 2,3-dimethyl-1,3-butadiene under TiO2 photoelectrochemical or electrochemical conditions to give spirocyclic cyclohexenes
electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the electron-rich arylalkene (anethole/arylidene cycloalkane) to its radical cation, which acts as the dienophile; the cyclohexene radical cation intermediate is then reduced back (at cathode or by another substrate molecule in a chain) to give the neutral cycloadduct

key_intermediates.name: distonic cyclohexene radical cation (after C–C bond formation with diene)

electron_flow: Anode (or photoexcited TiO2 hole) oxidizes the electron-rich styrene/arylidene cycloalkane to a radical cation, which undergoes stepwise [4+2] addition with 2,3-dimethyl-1,3-butadiene to give a cyclohexene radical cation; back-electron transfer (from cathode, TiO2 conduction-band electron, or a neutral substrate in a chain) regenerates the neutral Diels–Alder adduct.

probe_reagents.name: β-methylstyrene (non-substituted dienophile control)
probe_reagents.observation: Productive under TiO2 photoelectrochemical conditions (61%) but 0% under simple electrochemical conditions, showing photoelectrochemistry enables otherwise unreactive substrates

probe_reagents.name: β-methylanethole (1)
probe_reagents.observation: Trace under TiO2/UV (9%) but 43% under electrochemistry; β-substitution introduces steric hindrance disfavoring the radical cation cycloaddition

probe_reagents.name: arylidene cyclopropane (4)
probe_reagents.observation: Totally unreactive under both conditions, attributed to instability/strain of the proposed tertiary radical cation

probe_reagents.name: arylidene cyclobutane (5)
probe_reagents.observation: Spiro adduct 9 formed in 61% (photo) / 66% (EC), showing reduced steric hindrance vs gem-dimethyl analog enables reaction

probe_reagents.name: arylidene cyclopentane (6) and cyclohexane (7)
probe_reagents.observation: Less productive (10: 0–5%; 11: 31–56%), indicating ring-size dependent steric/electronic effects

notes: Mechanism (Figure 1) is the established radical cation Diels–Alder pathway adopted from prior literature; no direct mechanistic probes (TEMPO, KIE, CV, DFT) were performed in this work. The study focuses on substrate scope to test steric/electronic effects of β-substitution. TiO2 photoelectrochemistry is invoked as advantageous because both single-electron oxidation and reduction occur at the same semiconductor surface, enabling a productive chain/back-ET pathway not accessible under simple constant-potential electrolysis for some substrates.
